content.text: Audit logging for tunnel-accessed resources
is the practice of capturing
tamper-resistant, structured logs

for every MCP call that flows through a tunnel—including the tool name, arguments (hashed), timestamp, agent identity, and outcome—so you can reconstruct a complete audit trail for compliance, incident investigation, or forensic analysis. The key requirement is that logging happens on
both sides

: the Anthropic/Cloud side (agent-side view in the Console) and your tunnel gateway (network-side view), because you need both to reconstruct the full picture of what the agent requested and what your internal system executed.

attrs.alt: Audit logging for tunnel-accessed resources concept diagram

content.text: What it is

Audit logging for tunnel-accessed resources captures:
-
Tool name
: Which MCP tool was called (e.g.,
get_customer_data
Arguments hash

: Hash of the tool arguments (to avoid logging sensitive data directly).
-
Timestamp

: When the call occurred (ISO 8601).
-
Agent identity

: Which agent initiated the call (agent ID, workspace).
-
Session ID

: Unique session identifier for the agent run.
-
User identity

: Human identity that triggered the agent (if SSO is enabled).
-
Outcome

: Success/failure, error message, response size.
-
Network details
: Source IP, destination IP, bytes transferred.

{
  "timestamp": "2026-06-22T19:50:00Z",
  "tool_name": "get_customer_data",
  "arguments_hash": "sha256:e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855",
  "agent_id": "agent_database_003",
  "session_id": "sess_abc123",
  "user_id": "user_123",
  "source_ip": "10.0.1.50",
  "destination_ip": "10.0.2.100",
  "bytes_transferred": 1024,
  "outcome": "success",
  "error_message": null
}
Why it matters

Without audit logging:
-
No compliance coverage

: You can't prove to auditors which resources were accessed.
-
No incident reconstruction

: You can't trace what happened during a security breach.
-
No forensic analysis

: You can't investigate suspicious agent behavior.
-
No visibility
: You don't know which tools are being used most frequently.

With audit logging:
-
Compliance coverage

: You can prove access patterns for SOC 2, HIPAA, ISO 27001.
-
Incident reconstruction

: You can trace the full audit trail from agent request to MCP execution.
-
Forensic analysis

: You can investigate suspicious behavior (e.g., agent accessed unexpected data).
-
Visibility
: You can monitor tool usage patterns and detect anomalies.
This is critical for
enterprise deployments
where security teams require audit logging as a prerequisite for MCP adoption.
Key requirement: Dual-side logging
The most important requirement is
logging on both sides

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Agent Side (Anthropic Console)                          │
│  • Shows agent-side view of MCP calls                    │
│  • Logs: tool name, arguments, timestamp, agent identity │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ↓
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Tunnel Gateway (Your Network)                            │
│  • Logs every MCP call that flows through the tunnel      │
│  • Logs: source IP, destination IP, bytes, outcome        │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ↓
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  MCP Server (Your Internal System)                        │
│  • Logs execution on your side                            │
│  • Logs: tool execution, parameters (scrubbed), result    │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
You need both to reconstruct a full audit trail

:
- Gateway logs every MCP call (network-side view).
- Sandbox server (if custom) logs execution on your side.
- Anthropic Console shows agent-side view.
If you only have one side, you can't reconstruct what the agent requested vs. what your system executed.
Logging best practices
Practice
Why
Log raw prompt input, tool outputs, agent decisions
Full context for forensic inspection
Timestamped JSON with hash chaining
Tamper-resistant logs (blockchain-style integrity)
Use append-only log store (WORM S3, Loki)
Logs can't be deleted or modified after writing
Scrub sensitive info from parameters
Don't log customer data directly; use hash instead
Log external API calls
If MCP server accesses external APIs, log those with high-level details
Stream logs to SIEM
Your SIEM should capture gateway + sandbox logs before claiming compliance
Enable metrics + alerts
Request counts, error counts, latency distributions for each tool
Implementation patterns
Pattern 1: Google Cloud MCP audit logging
Google Cloud MCP servers generate audit logs that record Data Access events:

# Enable audit logs for MCP (disabled by default)
gcloud services enable-data-access-logging \
  --service=mcp.googleapis.com \
  --log-type=DATA_READ
Note
: Data Access audit logs are disabled by default because they're large. You must explicitly enable them.
Pattern 2: PingOne Privilege MCP gateway
PingOne Privilege provides just-in-time access with full session visibility:

Admin Console → Activity → Session Logs
• Sessions list user, device, service, resource type (MCP)
• Mark sessions as audited after review

Admin Console → Activity → Activity Logs
• Filter by MCP Server application, user
• Inspect individual events (tool name, arguments, progress tokens)
• Use Event Information and Resources views for more context
Key feature
: Fine-grained access control at tool, prompt, and resource level.
Pattern 3: Anthropic tunnel gateway logs

Anthropic tunnel gateway logs every MCP call with:
- Tool name
- Arguments hash
- Timestamp
- Agent identity

# tunnel_gateway.py
import logging
import json
import hashlib

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger("tunnel_gateway")

def log_mcp_call(tool_name: str, arguments: dict, agent_id: str, session_id: str):
    # Hash arguments to avoid logging sensitive data
    args_hash = hashlib.sha256(json.dumps(arguments).encode()).hexdigest()
    
    log_entry = {
        "timestamp": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        "tool_name": tool_name,
        "arguments_hash": args_hash,
        "agent_id": agent_id,
        "session_id": session_id,
        "source_ip": get_source_ip(),
        "destination_ip": get_destination_ip()
    }
    
    logger.info(json.dumps(log_entry))
Then stream logs to your SIEM before claiming compliance coverage.
Pattern 4: Hash chaining (blockchain logs)
For tamper-resistant logs, use hash chaining:
This creates an
append-only log
where each entry is cryptographically linked to the previous one.
Responding to a suspected breach
If you detect suspicious audit logging patterns:
Review logs
: Inspect proxy, cloudflared, MCP server logs for the compromise window.
Stop the tunnel stack
: Halt all MCP calls through the tunnel.
Archive the tunnel
: Invalidates tunnel token and detaches domain.
Rotate credentials
: Re-provision fresh tunnel; rotate OAuth tokens and certificates.
Restore after review
: Only restore after verifying logs are clean.
Key takeaways
Audit logging =
tamper-resistant, structured logs
for every MCP call through a tunnel
Captures
tool name, arguments hash, timestamp, agent identity, session ID, outcome
Dual-side logging is critical
: Gateway logs + sandbox logs + Anthropic Console = full audit trail
Verify SIEM coverage
: Gateway + sandbox logs must be captured by your SIEM before claiming compliance
Use
hash chaining
for tamper-resistant logs (blockchain-style integrity)
Stream to SIEM
: Essential for compliance and incident investigation
Best Practices
When working with audit logging for tunnel-accessed resources, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
Common Mistakes to Avoid
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with audit logging for tunnel-accessed resources. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
Performance and Scalability
As your usage of audit logging for tunnel-accessed resources scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
Best Practices
Follow these best practices when working with audit logging for tunnel-accessed resources to maximize reliability and performance.
Start simple and iterate incrementally — validate your core approach before adding complexity.
Test with realistic inputs — use representative examples from your actual use case, not just toy examples.
Monitor in production — track latency, error rates, and output quality with structured logging and metrics.
Version control your configurations — treat prompts and settings like code with proper change tracking.
Handle errors gracefully — implement retry logic with exponential backoff for transient API errors.
Set appropriate timeouts — long-running requests should have explicit timeout limits to prevent hangs.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit produced content to catch quality drift as models are updated.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
These pitfalls catch most developers off guard. Learn from them upfront.
Treating LLM output as deterministic — outputs vary between runs. Always validate structure and handle unexpected formats.
Not accounting for token limits — conversations grow over time. Implement context truncation strategies before hitting limits.
Over-engineering before validation — build the simplest thing that works, then optimize based on real measurements.
Ignoring rate limits — API throttling causes failures under load. Implement request queuing and rate-limit-aware retry logic.
Hardcoding prompts in source code — separate prompt text from code to enable rapid iteration without deployments.
Performance and Scalability Considerations
At scale, audit logging for tunnel-accessed resources introduces specific performance challenges that require deliberate design decisions.
Latency: Claude Haiku responds in 1-5 seconds; Sonnet in 5-15 seconds; Opus in 15-30+ seconds. Choose the right model for your latency budget.
Throughput: Use async clients (AsyncAnthropic in Python, async/await in TypeScript) for concurrent requests rather than sequential blocking calls.
Cost optimization: Prompt caching reduces costs by 90% on repeated prefixes. The Message Batches API gives 50% discount for offline workloads.
Streaming: Use streaming responses to reduce perceived latency — users see output immediately rather than waiting for complete generation.
Connection pooling: Reuse HTTP connections with a persistent client instance rather than creating a new client per request.
Security and Reliability
Production deployments require attention to security boundaries and reliability engineering.
Never log API keys — rotate credentials immediately if they appear in logs, error messages, or version control history.
Validate and sanitize inputs — prompt injection attacks can exfiltrate data or override instructions if user inputs are embedded directly.
Implement circuit breakers — automatically stop sending requests when error rates exceed threshold to prevent cascading failures.
Audit all AI-generated actions — log every API call, input, and output with correlation IDs for debugging and compliance.
Summary

Audit logging for tunnel-accessed resources is a foundational aspect of building reliable Claude-powered applications. The patterns covered here — careful validation, incremental iteration, proper error handling, and performance-aware design — apply broadly across all Claude integrations. Master these fundamentals and you will be well-equipped to tackle more complex scenarios as your application evolves.
